narrative: As a white male applicant with a part-time enrollment plan and a lower-middle family income background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 27 (well under the 36.70 average), Year 1 grade decile of 4 (versus 5.71 average), and Year 3 decile of 1 (far below 5.53 average)—and output a 40% predicted probability that you would fail the bar exam. This elevated risk threshold led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar from the outset. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 academic decile ranking at the bottom of your school, which strongly signaled potential struggles in advanced coursework compared to peers who ranked higher. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your family income bracket, which nudged the prediction toward success, as students from similar lower-middle backgrounds often outperform expectations through grit and determination. Your Year 1 decile, while not top-tier, also mildly supported a passing prediction by showing some early promise relative to averages. Overall, these top three elements—dominated by that low Year 3 performance—outweighed other aspects like your LSAT and GPA in shaping the model's cautious stance, especially since part-time students make up just 7.3% of the dataset and face steeper odds. Compared to typical admitted students, your metrics fell short in key academic deciles, placing you in a higher-risk group for bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a modest background who've clearly worked hard to get here—many in similar situations turn things around. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly above average, strengthening your recent academic record through postgraduate courses or a master's program, and perhaps exploring full-time options to align with the majority profile. Consider schools with holistic reviews that weigh personal stories heavily, and gather strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience. With targeted improvements, you can reshape your profile for future success. Keep pushing—you've got this.
